original_abstract:
This paper provides a comprehensive mechanistic investigation into anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization reactions catalyzed by 9-mesityl-10-methylacridinium (Mes-Acr+). Utilizing a combination of transient absorption spectroscopy, steady-state fluorescence, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations, the authors elucidate the complex interplay between the photoredox catalyst and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) cocatalysts such as thiophenol and diphenyl disulfide. 

Laser flash photolysis enabled the direct detection of alkene cation radical intermediates, confirming a single-electron transfer mechanism over alternative exciplex pathways. The study identifies that while all studied alkenes are oxidized by the singlet excited state of Mes-Acr+ on a nanosecond timescale, those with lower oxidation potentials may also involve the triplet state. A vital finding is the high-speed oxidation of the persistent acridinyl radical (Mes-Acr·) by the phenylthiyl radical, a step that effectively couples the photoredox and HAT catalytic cycles. 

Comparative analysis reveals that diphenyl disulfide functions as a pre-catalyst that undergoes direct photolytic cleavage to enter the same catalytic manifold as thiophenol. DFT results suggests that thiophenol is a significantly more efficient HAT donor than 2-phenylmalononitrile due to lower structural reorganization energies. Finally, the observation of a reversible, off-cycle acridinium-thiolate adduct during the reaction suggests that proton transfer may be a key rate-limiting factor in these transformations. These insights provide a robust framework for the future design and optimization of acridinium-based photocatalytic systems.
humanized_abstract:
The mechanistic insights into anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization catalyzed by 9-mesityl-10-methylacridinium (Mes-Acr+) were systematically investigated in this study. By employing transient absorption spectroscopy, steady-state fluorescence and density functional theory (DFT) computations, the photochemical interactions between the photoredox catalyst and the hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) activators including thiophenol and diphenyl disulfide were fully elucidated.

We have for the first time, demonstrated the existence of alkene cation radical species by using laser flash photolysis. The investigations presented in this communication show that all the alkene substrates investigated are oxidized by the singlet excited state of Mes-Acr+ on the nanosecond time scale, while some of the substrates can be also oxidized by the triplet Mes-Acr*. Most importantly, we have demonstrated high-speed oxidation of the persistent acridinyl radical (Mes-Acr·) by the phenylthiyl (PhSH) radical and our investigations hence provide a clear-cut evidence that the photoredox and HAT catalytic cycles can be strongly coupled through this high-speed oxidation reaction.

Diphenyl disulfide is demonstrated through comparative studies to act as a pre-catalyst that upon direct photolytic cleavage serves to catalyze reactions initiated by thiophenol. DFT analysis points towards thiophenol being a far more efficient HAT agent than 2-phenylmalononitrile in these reactions due to lower energy costs associated with structural reorganization. Further insight comes from the demonstration that the reaction cycle is reversible and that the off-cycle acridinium-thiolate adduct of catalyst and reductant is stable and forms reversibly, thereby suggesting that the rate determining step for catalyst turnover is proton transfer. Overall, the findings here provide a solid foundation from which to design more functional acridinium-based photocatalysts.

document.output:
The mechanistic insights into anti-Markovnikov alkene hydrofunctionalization catalyzed by 9-mesityl-10-methylacridinium (Mes-Acr+) were systematically investigated in this study. Diphenyl disulfide is demonstrated through comparative studies to act as a pre-catalyst that upon direct photolytic cleavage serves to catalyze reactions initiated by thiophenol.
